articles.title: Netanyahu says Israel 'acted alone' in attack on Iranian gas field
articles.body: Gas prices, which had already been rising since the start of the conflict, surged in response to the Iranian attack on Qatar. The UK benchmark peaked briefly at almost 183p per therm on Thursday before easing back to 154.8p, an 11.3% increase from Wednesday's level. European prices also rose by more than 10%.

articles.title: Strait of Hormuz: Which ships are passing through?
articles.body:
Trump 24-karat gold coin approved by hand-picked federal panel

The coin, intended to mark the nation's 250th birthday, should be "as large as possible", says one panel member.

articles.title: US considers lifting sanctions on some Iranian oil
articles.body: "To put it mildly, this is bananas," said David Tannenbaum, director of Blackstone Compliance Services, a consultancy specialising in maritime sanctions. "Essentially we're allowing Iran to sell oil, which could then be used to fund the war effort."

articles.title: Denmark planned to blow up Greenland runways if US invaded, reports say
articles.body: Soon afterwards, a small military contingent of Danish, French, German, Norwegian and Swedish soldiers was flown to the Greenland capital Nuuk and Kangerlussuaq, where there is an airport.

articles.title: Iran: Teenager among first to be executed over anti-government protests
articles.body: Tasnim, a semi-official news agency associated with Iran's Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC), reported that Mohammadi, Mehdi Ghasemi, and Saeed Davoudi were found guilty of killing two police officers in separate attacks in Qom.

articles.title: Taylor Frankie Paul's Bachelorette season pulled after abuse allegations
articles.body: A spokesperson for Disney, which owns ABC, said it made the decision to pull the season "in light of the newly released video just surfaced today", referring to the footage, which was published by TMZ.

articles.title: Norway crown princess to address Epstein links as son's rape trial closes
articles.body: The first rape charge, dating back to 2018, took place at his the crown prince and princess's official Skaugum estate outside Oslo. Commentators saw his trial as the biggest scandal Norway's royals had ever had to face.

articles.title: US civil rights leader Cesar Chavez accused of sexual abuse
articles.body: Dolores Huerta said that, in the 1960s, Chavez, who co-founded the United Farm Workers union (UFW) with her, once "manipulated and pressured" her into having sex, and on another occasion forced her.

articles.title: Spain's king welcomes Mexico's World Cup invite after 'abuse' comments
articles.body: "There are things that, when we study them, with our present-day criteria, our values, obviously cannot make us feel proud," Felipe said while visiting an exhibition on indigenous Mexican women in Madrid's National Archaeological Museum.

articles.title: DR Congo and Rwanda agree to ease tensions after talks in US
articles.body: Days after the signing ceremony, the M23 entered the Congolese city of Uvira near the Burundi border in the biggest escalation of the conflict in months. It later pulled out under US pressure, although it still controls large parts of eastern DR Congo, including the region's two biggest cities, Goma and Bukavu.

articles.title: Three Palestinian women killed in beauty salon during Iranian missile attack
articles.body: Wafa identified those killed in the salon as Mais Ghazi Masalmeh, 17, Sahira Rizq Masalmeh, 50, and Amal Sobhi Abdel Karim Matawa' Masalmeh, 36.

articles.title: BTS Seoul concert: Capital braces for the Army as band returns
articles.body: Ami Ostrovskaia, who moved to Seoul from Russia last year to study, is one of the lucky ones. "BTS is the reason I'm here," she says. "It's why I started learning about Korean history, culture, food, sports, and the language."

articles.title: Iran war: Gas shortage risks pushing India towards polluting fuels
articles.body: India's environment ministry has told state pollution boards to let restaurants and hotels temporarily switch to biomass (wood, dried crops, animal manure), fuel pellets, kerosene and coal for a month, prioritising cooking gas for households and essential sectors.

articles.title: Nowruz: Iran prepare for Persian new year under the shadow of war
articles.body: To Iranians, Nowruz represents their history, national character and tradition. Persians, Parsis, Kurds, Armenians, Azerbaijanis, Tajiks, Kazakhs, Uzbeks and other cultures celebrate the festival and have their own traditions surrounding it.

articles.title: Kenya food crisis: Desperate people turn to the gingerbread tree in Turkana
articles.body: According to experts at the local National Drought Management Authority, the rains have been uneven, with some parts of Turkana receiving little to none, while the rainfall remains unpredictable and insufficient to offset the impact of the last two failed rainy seasons.

articles.title: Are US and Israel in lockstep in Iran war? Deciphering Trump's post after gas field attacks
articles.body: On Thursday morning, Alex Gandler, spokesman for the Israeli embassy in London, told the BBC, "We are very much aligned on most or all of our goals regarding the Islamic regime in Iran, the IRGC [Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps], their ballistic and nuclear programmes," and "We want the same thing."

articles.title: How Europe sleepwalked into yet another energy crisis
articles.body: Italian Prime Minister, Giorgia Meloni, said last week: "With the outbreak of the crisis in the Middle East, the issue of energy prices has clearly become even more important, which is why, at European level, we are also calling for the urgent suspension of the application of the ETS to electricity production."

articles.title: As Cuba struggles with power cuts, how is the island holding out?
articles.body:
Cuba's grid operator has restored intermittent power after the national electric grid collapsed on 16 March. It comes amid a US-imposed oil blockade that has crippled the island's already struggling fuel, food, and medicine shortages.

Several small groups of residents across Havana have in recent days banged pots in protest against extended blackouts. The series of widespread outages that last for hours or days sparked a rare violent protest in northern Cuba on 14 March, where a building was set ablaze.

President Donald Trump has in recent months hinted at a possible takeover of the island and during a White House event in early March said the island was "ready to fall".

The BBC's Cuba correspondent Will Grant reports from the island.

articles.title: Gabbard faces scrutiny over Iran nuclear intelligence claims
articles.body:
The US Director of National Intelligence, Tulsi Gabbard, was questioned over what Democrats perceive as a discrepancy between White House and intelligence community claims on Iran's nuclear enrichment capabilities.

She wrote in her pre-written opening statement to the Senate Intelligence Committee; 'As a result of Operation Midnight Hammer, Iran’s nuclear enrichment program was obliterated. There has been no efforts since then to try to rebuild their enrichment capability.'

But in her oral statement she said, 'prior to Operation Epic Fury, the IC assesses Iran was trying to recover from the severe damage to its nuclear infrastructure sustained during the 12 Day War and continued to refuse to comply with its nuclear obligations with the IAEA, refusing them access to key facilities.'

When pressed by Democratic senators, she said that she changed her statement to save time.

articles.title: Iditarod dog sled race ends in repeat win for Jessie Holmes
articles.body:
Jessie Holmes has won his second consecutive Iditarod Trail Sled Dog Race in Nome, Alaska. The former reality TV star completed the roughly 1,000-mile (1,609-kilometer) course in nine days, seven hours, and 32 minutes.

As this year’s winner, Holmes received an $80,000 prize. He also earned an additional $5,500 in cash and $4,500 in gold nuggets for sweeping every special award along the trail.

Holmes made his Iditarod debut in 2018 and has competed every year since, earning five top-10 finishes in addition to his two victories.

articles.title: Venezuelans celebrate historic WBC victory over USA
articles.body:
Venezuelans around the world are celebrating a historic baseball victory after Venezuela captured its first-ever World Baseball Classic title.

The team defeated the United States 3–2, with Eugenio Suárez breaking the tie in the top of the ninth inning.

The win comes at a time of political turmoil for the country following the US government's seizure of Venezuelan President Nicolás Maduro during a military raid in January.
